source: apify/instagram-scraper (own account: manual fallback — Instagram blocks scraper access to this small account)

own.bio: Strange but true stories, told quietly. New odd hour, every week.

posts.caption: In 2021, one engineer typed one wrong command and 3.5 billion people lost the internet in a single instant. Not hacked. Not attacked. Just one wrong line of code during a routine maintenance check. Facebook's own engineers couldn't even log in to fix it, their access tools were on the same servers that just vanished. They had to physically drive to data centers and override servers by hand. One by one. 6 hours. $60 million lost every single hour. And the exact same vulnerability? Still exists right now. The internet is more fragile than you think.

2021 mein ek engineer ne ek galat command type ki aur 3.5 billion logon ka internet ek pal mein band ho gaya. Hack nahi hua. Attack nahi hua. Bas ek routine maintenance check ke dauran ek galat line of code. 6 ghante. Har ghante 60 million dollar ka nuksaan. Internet utna strong nahi jitna hum sochte hain.

There is a sound that only 2% of people can hear. Scientists have studied it for 50 years and still have no answer. It's called The Hum — and the leading theory is that it's the Earth itself vibrating at a frequency most humans aren't built to detect. A woman heard it for 11 years, moved across the country to escape it, and it followed her to the new house. Stop right now, close your eyes, and listen. What do you hear?

We call space the final frontier. But we've barely explored our own planet. Earth is stranger than anything we've found out there — and most people have no idea. From perfect solar eclipses to oceans deeper than mountains are tall, this is the planet you thought you knew.

In 1974, a man calculated the exact day he would die and spent a year preparing for it. What happened next is stranger than the prediction itself. Frank was obsessed with numerology. He told his family, quit his job, and gave away everything he owned. When the day came and went without incident, he thought he'd beaten fate. He hadn't.

They had to physically drive to data centers and override servers by hand. One by one. 6 hours. $60 million lost every single hour. And the exact same vulnerability? Still exists right now. recentPosts.caption: It seems that Midas have stumbled over a rock and fell on the ground. Surely nothing bad can come out of that... 👀

On the vast, cold seas with endless waves in all directions, there is one constant companion: the stars. Whenever they were visible, sailors knew they could find their way. Their guidance meant ships could dare to cross the oceans with nothing more than keen eyesight and the ability to read the signs in the sky. Just like the brave sailors aboard Chinese junks that can be seen on the July page of our 12,026 Human Era calendar, who left familiar shores to cross oceans and head into the unknown.

War costs far more than weapons and destroyed buildings. It leaves countries poorer, disrupts education, drives migration, and passes trauma to future generations. Some of its highest costs are paid decades later.
If you don't know, don't worry! You'll find out soon on our Nightshift channel... 🌚

Things that can help during a panic attack: 
1. Breathe. Practice abdominal breathing: Breathe slowly from your abdomen through your nose to reduce symptoms of hyperventilation.
2.  Stay in the present. Staying in the present and focussing on external objects will help minimize the attention you might give to troublesome physical symptoms or catastrophic thoughts. If possible, you might try actually touching objects nearby to reinforce staying in the immediate present or use the 5-4-3-2-1 exerci

You've heard about synchronized swimming, but have you heard about synchronized rocket launching?

Don't miss out, and join us on this space adventure! Get Star Birds, which we developed together with our friends at @toukana_interactive on Steam!
What exactly is caffeine, and how does it affect your brain? Its special chemistry is what makes it helpful in keeping you focused, but it also comes with a cost.
Caption this Nightshift scene
Yesterday we talked about the top 3 microbe predators, and today we share the top 3 memes from that video 😌

There is a hidden world filled with monsters you never heard of, from predators who spit acid and shoot harpoons, to armored hunters that devour their prey alive! Deep in the oceans, and even in fresh water from puddles and lakes, these microscopic super predators remained hidden in plain sight, and evolved over the last 2 billion years.

What are the deadliest predators of the microworld, and what kind of bizarre biological weapons have they evolved over the past billions of years? Watch our fu
When someone opens snacks in the office
What could 1% of the richest countries’ income achieve? From malaria vaccines and clean water to renewable energy, a surprisingly small fraction of global wealth could transform millions of lives.

competitors.bio: Discover the most incredible experiences from around the world 🌎✈️ Business: info@damiermedia.com

recentPosts.caption:
Ulaman Eco Luxury Resort in Bali is surrounded by jungle, waterfalls, and a river that runs through the property.⁠
⁠
The rooms are surrounded by trees, with natural pools, gardens, and walkways that make the whole place feel far removed from the outside world.⁠
⁠
Built with sustainability in mind, Ulaman shows how architecture can create something that feels completely different from a typical resort.⁠
⁠
It’s easy to see why visitors compare it to the world of Avatar.⁠
⁠⁠
By: @ulamanbali @offtos

Semana Santa is an annual religious tradition held in Seville, Spain, during the week leading up to Easter.⁠
⁠
For centuries, local brotherhoods have honored the Passion of Christ through processions that bring together faith, art, and history.⁠
⁠
The celebration is one of the best-known events in Spain, with visitors arriving from around the world to experience Seville’s unique atmosphere during Holy Week.⁠
⁠
By: @sbam_design, @sebasvalencia_fotografia, @createdbyalaritammsalu, alienganímedes,

For a few months each year, Mykines becomes a remarkable wildlife destination. Hundreds of thousands of puffins return to breed, temporarily outnumbering the people who call the island home. ⁠
⁠
By the end of summer, they’re gone again, and the island returns to its quiet pace.

When Casa Milà was unveiled in the early 1900s, it looked unlike anything people had ever seen.⁠
⁠
Designed by Antoni Gaudí, the building challenged traditional architecture with flowing curves, organic shapes, and almost no straight lines. Inspired by nature, every detail was designed to feel alive, from the wave-like stone façade to the sculptural rooftop chimneys.⁠
⁠
More than 100 years later, Casa Milà continues to attract visitors from around the world and stands as one of Barcelona's most

Some places are more than just beautiful landscapes.⁠
⁠
For the Wii Towai Tribe of Papua New Guinea, this waterfall is a sacred place deeply connected to their culture, traditions, and identity. Tribal members have protected it for generations, following customs that reflect respect for both nature and their ancestors.⁠
⁠
To visitors, it may look like an unusual tradition, but to the community, it's simply part of who they are.⁠
⁠
By: @adrift.vision @dianahdaisy

If you've ever seen a blue jay that looked half gray and half blue, you were probably looking at a bird in the middle of one of nature's most fascinating transformations.⁠
⁠
As young blue jays grow, they gradually replace their soft juvenile feathers with the vibrant blue plumage adults are known for. For a short period, both generations of feathers can appear at the same time, creating a striking patchwork of colors.⁠
⁠
It's a brief stage that most people never notice, but it offers a rare glim

This is what the end of the Great Wall of China looks like.⁠
⁠
While most people imagine it stretching endlessly through mountains, deserts, and valleys, it actually has a final point where stone meets the sea.⁠
⁠
At Shanhai Pass, also known as “Old Dragon’s Head,” the wall extends into the Bohai Sea, marking the easternmost edge of one of the world’s greatest structures.⁠

Every summer in Japan, forests and rivers quietly turn into something unforgettable.⁠
⁠
Fireflies light up the night to communicate during mating season. As a result, entire landscapes begin to glow, almost like a natural “light concert” happening deep in the wild.⁠
⁠
Local viewing spots protect this seasonal event, allowing people to experience it without disturbing the fireflies’ natural rhythm.⁠
⁠
By: @settouchiguide

Some libraries are more than just places to read. They’re works of art.⁠
⁠
Around the world, some libraries feel like stepping into a movie set, from grand historic halls filled with ancient books to futuristic spaces that look like something from another planet.⁠
⁠
Each one tells its own story through architecture, silence, and the knowledge it holds within its walls.⁠
⁠
Which one would you visit first?⁠
⁠
By: @calvinseng49 @olharesdowill @bildmomente @photo_macjac @trev.takes.photos @itsehsanh

Koalas are known for spending most of their lives asleep, but there’s a reason behind it. Their diet consists of eucalyptus leaves, which provide very little energy.⁠
⁠
To make the most of that, koalas can sleep up to 20 hours a day and avoid wasting energy whenever they can. That even extends to mating season, when some choose to stop searching for a mate and return to sleep instead. ⁠
⁠
By: @eclipse.chasers @diefotoweltvonfabian @carolinecedam @maria_ch__ Chris F, Anthony's images

The Leaning Tower of Pisa wasn’t supposed to look like this.⁠
⁠
Built between the 12th and 14th centuries, the tower began tilting shortly after construction due to soft, unstable ground beneath its foundation.⁠
⁠
At one point in history, it leaned so dangerously that engineers feared it could collapse. But thanks to modern restoration efforts, the tower was stabilized and saved. ⁠
⁠
Today, it still stands proudly in its famous tilt, attracting millions of visitors every year.⁠
⁠
By: teeixi22

In parts of Japan, visitors are often greeted by an unexpected sight: wild deer bowing their heads just like humans.⁠
⁠
The behavior is especially common in Nara, where generations of deer have learned that a polite bow is often rewarded with special deer crackers from tourists.⁠
⁠
Over time, what began as a simple association between food and people turned into one of Japan’s most charming wildlife traditions.⁠
⁠
Today, thousands of visitors travel to Nara each year to experience these surprisi

competitors.bio: Step into wonder and find your inner explorer with National Geographic 🌎

recentPosts.caption:
There's more to the story of Pompeii than just destruction. Join Tom Hiddleston as he traces the stories of those who experienced the eruption and those who survived.

#PompeiiOutOfTime with @twhiddleston premieres July 22 on @NatGeoTV. Streaming on @DisneyPlus and @hulu.

One bite is all it takes for this spider's trap to launch an ant into its web. The newly discovered ballista spider builds a sophisticated spring-loaded silk snare, using a hunting strategy unlike any scientists have documented in a spider before.

Learn more about this arachnid and its prey at the link in bio.

Video by Ajay Narendra, Macquarie University

Several books of the New Testament refer to Jesus' brothers and sisters. Still, interpretations of the original Greek in which the Gospels were written and historical evidence have led to different conclusions about who those relatives were. While some scholars point to census records from Roman Egypt that shed light on family structures at the time, the question of whether Jesus was Mary's only child remains one of Christianity's longest-running debates.

Dive deeper into the questions surround

Photos by @joelsartore | The transparent skin of the sun glass frog gives us an impressive view of the inner workings of this amphibian, with its internal organs, bones, and muscles on full display. The remarkable frogs reside along streams and rivers in the tropical forests of Ecuador, Colombia, and Panama. These photos were taken @centrojambatu. To see more species featured in the Photo Ark, follow me, @joelsartore.  #PhotoArk

Whether he's in nature or on the dance floor, there's one food @robertirwinphotography never leaves the house without 🥕 

Watch a new episode of #DWTSNextPro tonight at 8/7c on @abc. Stream next day on @DisneyPlus and @hulu.

One, two paws at a time, this red panda’s brightly colored fur stands out among the greenery as it moves through the branches at the Padmaja Naidu Himalayan Zoo in Darjeeling, India. It’s one of the few mammals that can climb down trees headfirst, using rotating hind feet, curved claws, and a bushy tail for balance.

Video by Munmun Dhalaria @moonpeakfilms

Skydiver Gabriel C. Brown crossed the face of the sun in a carefully timed jump over Arizona's Sonoran Desert, giving photographer Andrew McCarthy (@cosmic_background) the chance to capture this remarkable image. To create the surreal backdrop, McCarthy used a modified telescope to reveal the sun's chromosphere, then combined multiple exposures to produce the final photograph.

Some sharks navigate across entire ocean basins, covering distances comparable to Earth's circumference. Hidden within their snouts is the sensory system that helps guide these extraordinary journeys. But some species are adjusting their migrations—National Geographic Explorer Arzucan Askin (@zuzu_askin) explains why.

You can still be a sea turtle even if you're not a Taurus, we won't tell anyone.

National Geographic's #Sharkfest is now streaming on @DisneyPlus and @hulu, including #HammerheadSharksUpClose with @bertiegregory

Without ropes or safety gear, Alex Honnold ascended the 3,000-foot (914-meter) southwest face of El Capitan in California’s Yosemite National Park. Director and photographer @jimmychin captured Honnold on the granite wall during the climb, a feat that followed nearly a decade of preparation and was documented in Chin's film Free Solo.

From wildlife to wonder, explore more iconic images from the National Geographic archive at the link in bio.

During Since, an annual Quechua planting tradition, two farmers in the Peruvian village of Huaylacucho cover each other in corn flour. Photographer and National Geographic Explorer Florence Goupil (@florence.goupil) documented the playful ritual after growing up hearing stories about her grandmother's participation. “It was the first time I was able to see, with my own eyes, what my grandmother’s traditions looked like,” Goupil says.

For millennia, treatments for pain have been administered with a one-size-fits-all approach. It wasn’t until recently that experts officially acknowledged one radical truth: Women experience pain differently than men. They’re also more likely to experience chronic pain conditions and don’t respond as well to painkillers like ibuprofen, steroids, and opioids.
 
So how can women find relief? Learn more about why women experience pain differently—as well as evolving approaches to treatment—at the l

competitors.bio:
A window to our world 🌎
This is a commercial channel from BBC Studios.

recentPosts.caption:
Retiring at 100 years old? Not a chance! Here’s a sneak peek of Sir David Attenborough working on the narration for the upcoming BLUE PLANET III.

#DavidAttenborough100 #DavidAttenborough #BluePlanetIII

How do you attract a female? 🦌🦕

The most attractive are usually the biggest and hairiest with prominent features. In reproduction, they pass their genes to the next generation, who inherit more exaggerated attributes. Over millions of years, animal traits modify for better mating chances.

Find out where to watch Evolution via the link in our bio 🔗💚

#Evolution #ChrisPackham #Nature

Someone’s got serious aim 🎯
 
An archerfish doesn’t chase its prey. Instead, it fires a powerful jet of water with remarkable precision, knocking unsuspecting insects straight into the water. One perfectly placed shot is all it takes before the fish darts in to claim its meal. 
 
Celebrate 20 years of Planet Earth with us. Find out where to watch the full episode via our link in bio 💚

#PlanetEarth #DavidAttenborough #PE20 #PEIII

Penises come in all shapes and sizes 🍆

Shells prevented reptiles from fertilising their eggs, yet evolution created a solution: the penis. Reptiles were among the first animals to have them, but they weren’t the last. For life on land, it meant sex as we know it had been invented.

Find out where to watch Evolution via the link in our bio 🔗💚

#Evolution #ChrisPackham #Nature

🦦 Otter vs otter 🦦

Despite their cute reputation, fierce otter fights aren’t uncommon. Clashes over territories, food supplies and mating disputes can break out and become intense. Some end in serious injury, or even death.

EarthCapture by @jbucketslist
.
.
.
.
#Otters #Wildlife #Nature

It’s World Chimpanzee Day 🐵

Chimps share DNA with humans, but Dr Jane Goodall discovered other similarities making them our closest living relatives. They use tools, hold hands, embrace one another and use non-verbal communication like humans too. 

#EarthCapture via Shutterstock 📸

1. Patrick Rolands
2. Cheryl Ramalho
3. Nadine Wagner
4. Andrew Lilley
5. Gudkov Andrey
.
.
.
.
#WorldChimpanzeeDay #JaneGoodall #Nature

Warthog snouts are survival tools 🐗

It’s used for sniffing, digging and foraging, but they must bend their legs to reach the ground. Another creature evolved a longer snout for the same purpose, and it developed a body shape we recognise today.

Find out where to watch Evolution via the link in our bio 🔗💚

#Evolution #ChrisPackham #Nature

Every lesson comes with a risk 🐻 🎣
 
With the prime fishing grounds already claimed, a young mother and her cub head upstream into fast flowing water, where powerful rapids threaten to sweep the cub away. Will the gamble pay off, or has she pushed her luck too far? 
 
Explore the world’s few remaining untouched landscapes with Eden: Untamed Planet. Find the full series via the link in our bio 🔗

#EdenUntamedPlanet #Alaska #BearCub #Salmon

Looks can be deceptive 🌈
 
The rainbow sea slug is a marine mollusk known for a striking neon appearance. Its glowing colours are for protection warning predators they are toxic to eat.
 
#EarthCapture by @jjrheams.photography
.
.
.
.
#Seaslug #Wildlife #Nature

One of nature’s greatest acts of partnership 🪺
 
To give their chicks the best possible start, a female hornbill walls herself inside a tree hollow for months. With only a tiny opening between the, her survival and that of her family rests entirely in her partner’s care. 
 
Celebrate 20 years of Planet Earth with us. Find out where to watch the full episode via our link in bio 💚

#PlanetEarth #DavidAttenborough #PE20 #PEIII

Beware the ant army 🐜 

To consume prey, ants will swarm insects like crickets and caterpillars using sheer force of numbers. They can even overwhelm wounded chickens, goats and pigs. Nothing is off the menu… including @chrisgpackham2!

Find out where to watch Evolution via the link in our bio 🔗💚

#Evolution #ChrisPackham #Nature

You’ll never look at a tennis player’s racket arm the same way again 🎾
 
As a professional tennis player trains year after year, their skeleton adapts to the repeated impact of every shot. This causes the bones in their racket arm to become up to 20% thicker and stronger than the other and reduces the risk of breaking.
 
Watch the full clip and other videos from Secrets of the Human Body via the link in bio 🔗
 
#Tennis #Wimbeldon #RacketSport
